It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.

Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 47 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 1.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! Looking at the checkerboard pattern at the token columns:
- Rows 11-12: ccBcB
- Rows 13-15: BBYBc (Y is the top yellow shape)
- Row 16: BBcBc (bottom of top shape, gap at col 31)
- Rows 17-20: ccBcB
- Rows 30-32: BBcBc
- Rows 33-35: ccBcB
- Row 36: ccYcB (Y is the bottom shape's bridge)
- Row 37: BBYBc (bottom of bottom shape)
- Rows 38-40: BBcBc
The checkerboard pattern alternates between "ccBcB" and "BBcBc" (ignoring the yellow shapes). At the token columns:
- ccBcB: cols 21=c, 26=c, 31=B, 36=c, 41=B
- BBcBc: cols 21=B, 26=B, 31=c, 36=B, 41=c
So the checkerboard has a consistent pattern at the token columns. The "c" and "B" represent the checkerboard colors (charcoal and black).
Now, the tokens are at row 42 (horizontal) and rows 44-46 (vertical). The checkerboard at row 40 is "BBcBc", which means:
- Col 21: B (black)
- Col 26: B (black)
- Col 31: c (charcoal)
- Col 36: B (black)
- Col 41: c (charcoal)
But the tokens are black (B) or gray (w), not charcoal (c). So the checkerboard colors don't directly map to the token colors.
Wait, let me reconsider. The tokens are black (B) or light gray (w). The checkerboard is black (B) or charcoal (c). So "B" in the checkerboard corresponds to "B" (black) in the tokens, and "c" in the checkerboard corresponds to... what? Gray (w)?
If so, the checkerboard pattern at row 40 is "BBcBc", which would map to "BBwBw" for the tokens. But I've tried this pattern (H=('B', 'B', 'w', 'B', 'w')) and it didn't work.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine. The checkerboard pattern alternates every 2 rows. At rows 11-12, it's "ccBcB". At rows 13-15, it's "BBYBc" (with the top shape). At rows 17-20, it's "ccBcB". At rows 30-32, it's "BBcBc". At rows 33-35, it's "ccBcB". At rows 38-40, it's "BBcBc".
So the pattern alternates every 2 rows:
- Even row pairs (11-12, 17-20, 33-35): ccBcB
- Odd row pairs (13-16, 30-32, 38-40): BBcBc
Wait, that's not quite right. Let me look more carefully:
- Rows 11-12: ccBcB
- Rows 17-20: ccBcB
- Rows 33-35: ccBcB
- Rows 30-32: BBcBc
- Rows 38-40: BBcBc
So the pattern is:
- Rows 11-12: ccBcB
- Rows 13-16: BBcBc (with shape)
- Rows 17-29: ccBcB
- Rows 30-32: BBcBc
- Rows 33-37: ccBcB (with shape)
- Rows 38-40: BBcBc
The pattern alternates every 4 rows (or so). But this doesn't directly help me figure out the token pattern.
Let me think about this differently. What if the goal is to make the tokens match the "projection" of the yellow shapes onto the token row?
Top shape:
- Rows 13-15: YYYYY (5 wide, all yellow)
- Row 16: YccY (sides yellow, center gap)
The "projection" onto the token row would be the bottom row: YccY, which is ON, OFF, OFF, OFF, ON (for 5 tokens).
Bottom shape:
- Rows 33-35: Y   Y (sides yellow, center opening)
- Row 36: YYY Y (sides and center bridge yellow)
- Row 37: YYYYYY (6 wide, all yellow)
The "projection" onto the token row would be the top row: Y   Y, which is ON, OFF, OFF, OFF, OFF, ON (for 6 tokens). For 5 tokens: ON, OFF, OFF, OFF, ON.
Both shapes give the same pattern: ON, OFF, OFF, OFF, ON, which is ('B', 'w', 'w', 'w', 'B'). I've tried this for H with various V patterns, and it didn't work.
Hmm, let me try a completely different approach. What if the goal is to make the tokens match the "negative" of the shapes? That is, where the shape has a gap/opening, the token should be ON, and where the shape is solid, the token should be OFF.
Top shape gap: cols 31-33 (3 wide). For 5 tokens, this maps to token 3 (col 31).
Bottom shape opening: cols 30-33 (4 wide). For 5 tokens, this maps to tokens 2-3 (cols 26-31).
So:
- Horizontal tokens: token 3 ON -> w, w, w, B, w
- Vertical tokens: tokens 2-3 ON -> w, w, B, B, w
Let me try this!

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# New hypothesis: tokens should match the "negative" of the shapes
# Top shape gap: cols 31-33 -> token 3 ON (center token)
# Bottom shape opening: cols 30-33 -> tokens 2-3 ON (center 2 tokens)
# 
# Current state: H=('w', 'B', 'B', 'B', 'w'), V=('w', 'B', 'B', 'B', 'w')
# Target: H=('w', 'w', 'w', 'B', 'w'), V=('w', 'w', 'B', 'B', 'w')
#
# To get there:
# - H: toggle token 2 (B->w), toggle token 4 (B->w)
# - V: toggle token 2 (B->w), toggle token 4 (B->w)
#
# Token positions:
# H: token 1=(42,21), token 2=(42,26), token 3=(42,31), token 4=(42,36), token 5=(42,41)
# V: token 1=(44,21), token 2=(44,26), token 3=(44,31), token 4=(44,36), token 5=(44,41)

# First, let me set H to ('w', 'w', 'w', 'B', 'w')
# Current H is ('w', 'B', 'B', 'B', 'w')
# Need to toggle token 2 (B->w) and token 4 (B->w)
action([
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 42, 'col': 26},  # Toggle H token 2
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 42, 'col': 36},  # Toggle H token 4
])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
